Regulation Name: Computer Software Assurance for Production and Quality System Software
Summary: Final guidance detailing risk‑based Computer Software Assurance for production and quality system software, clarifying documentation expectations, testing approaches, and supplier controls used in medical device manufacturing and quality systems.
Healthcare Implications: Device makers, including AI‑enabled products, should revise validation plans to focus on intended use and risk, streamline evidence, and align Quality Management System procedures and supplier oversight to U.S. Food and Drug Administration expectations.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Digital Health Advisory Committee – Nov 6, 2025 Meeting (Generative AI-Enabled Digital Mental Health Devices)
Summary: U.S. Food and Drug Administration announced a Digital Health Advisory Committee meeting focused on generative‑AI digital mental‑health devices, opening a public docket and inviting expert input on benefits, risks, evidence, and oversight priorities.
Healthcare Implications: Signals near‑term policy attention; developers and clinicians can submit comments, preview evidence expectations, and anticipate future guidance affecting evaluation and post‑market monitoring of mental‑health AI tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Section 6(b) Market Study – AI Companion Chatbots
Summary: Federal Trade Commission issued compulsory orders to several 'AI companion' chatbot companies under Section 6(b), seeking information on data practices, safety safeguards, marketing claims, and impacts on minors and vulnerable users.
Healthcare Implications: Not binding for providers, but frames consumer‑protection risks; health systems and vendors should review chatbot use, disclosures, and guardrails, anticipating stricter expectations or enforcement around mental‑health harms.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: National Institute of Standards and Technology
Regulation Name: AI Standards “Zero Drafts” Pilot – Extended Outline for Documentation of AI Datasets and Models
Summary: National Institute of Standards and Technology released an outline for a forthcoming ‘zero draft’ standard on documentation of AI datasets and models, requesting input on transparency artifacts, provenance, curation, metadata, and quality reporting.
Healthcare Implications: Developers can align documentation templates early, easing future conformance; health systems may require such artifacts in procurement, improving reproducibility and auditability of clinical‑AI training datasets.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems

Regulation Name: Supporting Fairness and Originality in NIH Research Applications
Summary: Sets expectations for appropriate use of AI tools in NIH grant applications to preserve fairness and originality.
Healthcare Implications: Academic medical centers and investigators must adjust internal grant‑writing practices, disclosures, and oversight for AI tool use. Research offices should update policies to ensure originality and document acceptable AI assistance.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Hospital Inpatient Prospective Payment Systems and Long-Term Care Hospital Prospective Payment System FY 2026 Rule
Summary: Updates Medicare hospital inpatient and long‑term care hospital prospective payment system policies and rates for FY 2026; adjusts quality reporting and value‑based programs; and finalizes several policy clarifications and technical changes. Includes provisions affecting graduate medical education and hospital quality measures.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts hospital reimbursement, quality reporting, and documentation practices that interact with Electronic Health Records and AI‑enabled analytics. Changes to measure sets and program requirements shape how hospitals deploy clinical decision support and reporting automations. Budget and compliance teams should align AI/analytics workflows with updated payment and quality policies.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Issuing Body: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
Regulation Name: Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare Safety Program
Summary: Establishes an AI in Healthcare Safety Program within the Patient Safety Organization framework to identify harms and near‑misses from AI used in care. Provides resources and a pathway for learning from incidents, informed by EO 14110 and PSO confidentiality protections.
Healthcare Implications: Hospitals and PSOs gain a structured channel to report and analyze AI‑related safety events. Risk and quality teams should integrate AI incident capture, root‑cause analysis, and feedback loops into PSO workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; Contract Year 2026 Policy and Technical Changes to Medicare Advantage, Part D, Cost Plans, and PACE
Summary: Finalizes Contract Year 2026 policy and technical changes for Medicare Advantage (Part C), Part D, cost plans, and for the Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly, including adjustments to the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan, D‑SNPs, Star Ratings, utilization management, and other programmatic areas. Implements Inflation Reduction Act‑related changes and clarifies several beneficiary protections.
Healthcare Implications: Affects plan operations, prior authorization, and beneficiary communications–areas where plans increasingly use AI for triage, utilization review, and member outreach. Transparency and documentation requirements inform how AI‑assisted workflows must be governed. Providers may experience updated prior authorization processes and data exchange with Medicare Advantage/Part D plans.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Device Software Functions: Lifecycle Management & Marketing Submission Recommendations
Summary: Draft guidance outlining documentation and lifecycle considerations for marketing submissions involving AI‑enabled device software functions. Provides recommended content for safety/effectiveness evaluation and total product lifecycle practices.
Healthcare Implications: Helps device developers and provider‑innovators structure submissions and ongoing model management for AI‑enabled Software as a Medical Device. Supports iterative improvement with clarity on evidence and transparency expectations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Considerations for the Use of Artificial Intelligence To Support Regulatory Decision-Making for Drug & Biological Products
Summary: Draft guidance recommending practices for use of AI to generate information intended to support regulatory decisions for drugs/biologics, including transparency, validation, and risk management.
Healthcare Implications: Sponsors using AI in clinical development and CMC should align methods and documentation with U.S. Food and Drug Administration expectations, improving credibility and reproducibility of AI‑derived evidence.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Regulation Name: HTI-2 Final Rule – Trusted Exchange and Information Blocking Updates
Summary: Finalizes select proposals from HTI‑2 to support access, exchange, and use of Electronic Health Information. Amends information blocking regulations (including TEFCA‑related definitions) and advances Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement alignment.
Healthcare Implications: Strengthens nationwide interoperability and clarifies information‑blocking scope, enabling safer deployment of AI that depends on cross‑network data access. HIEs, EHRs, and providers should update policies and system behaviors accordingly.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs and CHIP; Hospital Inpatient Prospective Payment Systems for Acute Care Hospitals and LTCH PPS and FY 2025 Rates; Quality Programs Requirements
Summary: Revises hospital inpatient and long‑term care hospitals prospective payment system rates and policies for FY 2025, updates quality and reporting programs, and makes related technical changes. Includes adjustments to graduate medical education policies and clarifications across inpatient hospital payment provisions.
Healthcare Implications: Updates to quality reporting and documentation indirectly shape requirements for Electronic Health Record configuration, coding, and analytics. Hospitals using AI for documentation integrity, quality tracking, or measure abstraction should confirm alignment with the updated policies. Finance and compliance teams should assess downstream impacts on revenue cycle tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Predetermined Change Control Plans or Medical Devices
Summary: Announces availability of draft guidance on Predetermined Change Control Plans for medical devices, describing how sponsors can pre‑specify certain post‑market modifications and validation methods.
Healthcare Implications: Facilitates responsible iteration of AI‑enabled devices via pre‑authorized change plans, enabling faster updates with appropriate safeguards.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Health Breach Notification Rule – Final Rule Amendments
Summary: Amends the Health Breach Notification Rule to clarify coverage of health apps and connected devices, expand definitions (e.g., Personal Health Record related entities), and specify breach notification triggers and methods, with an effective date of July 29, 2024.
Healthcare Implications: Non‑Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act health apps and consumer digital health services handling sensitive data–including AI‑powered apps–must meet stricter breach notification duties. Health systems partnering with such apps should update vendor risk management and incident response plans.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Nondiscrimination in Health Programs and Activities
Summary: Finalizes CY 2024 updates to the Physician Fee Schedule and other Medicare Part B policies, including coding/payment updates, changes to the Quality Payment Program, and clarifications for telehealth and behavioral health services.
Healthcare Implications: Updates affect clinician reimbursement and reporting that interact with Electronic Health Record templates, clinical decision support, and AI‑assisted documentation. Telehealth and behavioral health provisions influence digital tools used in outpatient care and remote monitoring programs.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medical Devices; Laboratory Developed Tests
Summary: Amends U.S. Food and Drug Administration regulations to clarify that in vitro diagnostics, including those manufactured by laboratories, are devices under the FD&C Act; phases out general enforcement discretion and establishes staged compliance timelines over four years.
Healthcare Implications: Clinical laboratories and health systems must plan for registration/listing, quality system, and–where applicable–premarket review. AI‑assisted diagnostics using Laboratory Developed Tests should assess impact on validation evidence, labeling, and post‑market obligations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Regulation Name: Section 1557 Nondiscrimination in Health Programs and Activities
Summary: Finalizes regulations implementing Section 1557 of the ACA, strengthening nondiscrimination protections across covered health programs, including requirements for language access, effective communication, and protections against discrimination in clinical algorithms and telehealth.
Healthcare Implications: Covered entities must assess AI‑mediated tools for discriminatory impacts, ensure accessible digital services, and update policies, training, and notices. Implications span patient intake, triage, benefit design, and algorithm transparency.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Changes to MA and Part D for CY 2024 & 2025
Summary: Medicare hospital outpatient prospective payment system and ambulatory surgical center payment policies for the specified year, including packaging, device pass‑throughs, and quality reporting updates. Also includes technical corrections and clarifications.
Healthcare Implications: Outpatient payment and reporting changes impact how hospitals and ambulatory surgical center configure Electronic Health Record charge capture, quality dashboards, and any AI‑assisted coding or utilization management tools. Device and pass‑through updates may affect adoption pathways for AI‑enabled devices and diagnostics.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Regulation Name: Use of Online Tracking Technologies
Summary: Clarifies when identifiers collected via pixels/cookies/SDKs on patient‑facing sites/apps are Protected Health Information and how covered entities must manage such tools under HIPAA.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts telemetry stacks feeding analytics/AI (personalization, risk scoring). Requires revisiting tag configuration, consent/BAA posture, and logging; implementation should account for the court’s partial vacatur.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government; Patients & Public

Regulation Name: Marketing Submission Recommendations for a Predetermined Change Control Plan for AI/ML-Enabled Device Software Functions
Summary: Final guidance describing how to include a Predetermined Change Control Plan in marketing submissions for AI‑enabled device software functions. Explains PCCP components (Description of Modifications, Modification Protocol, Impact Assessment) and U.S. Food and Drug Administration's review expectations.
Healthcare Implications: Enables post‑market, pre‑authorized model updates without new submissions when within an approved Predetermined Change Control Plan – accelerating safe iteration of AI‑enabled devices. Hospitals and clinicians can expect timelier performance improvements with clearer labeling and change controls.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems

Regulation Name: Advisory Committee; Digital Health Advisory Committee; Addition to List of Standing Committees
Summary: Adds the Digital Health Advisory Committee to U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s list of standing advisory committees. The committee provides advice on digital health technologies, including Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning-enabled devices, remote patient monitoring, clinical decision support, and clinical trial technologies.
Healthcare Implications: Signals ongoing U.S. Food and Drug Administration engagement on AI and digital health issues affecting device evaluation and post‑market policies. Clinicians and developers can anticipate structured public input on digital health topics.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Regulation Name: Confidentiality of Substance Use Disorder Patient Records (42 CFR Part 2)
Summary: Aligns Part 2 confidentiality requirements more closely with Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)/HITECH pursuant to the CARES Act, enabling a single patient consent for future uses/disclosures; adds anti‑discrimination protections; harmonizes breach notification and enforcement provisions.
Healthcare Implications: AI analytics and decision support using SUD data must honor Part 2 consent and redisclosure restrictions. Developers and providers should segregate sensitive data and document lawful bases when training or operating models that involve SUD records.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; Advancing Interoperability and Improving Prior Authorization Processes
Summary: Improves electronic exchange of health data and streamlines prior authorization by requiring application programming interfaces (e.g., Prior Authorization Support, Patient Access, Payer‑to‑Payer), shorter prior authorization decision timeframes, and new metrics reporting for impacted payers.
Healthcare Implications: Directly affects AI‑assisted prior authorization and care coordination. Payers and providers must integrate Application Programming Interface (API)‑based PA workflows, which enable automation and analytics while preserving transparency, timelines, and patient access.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Regulation Name: CMS-0057-F – Interoperability & Prior Authorization Final Rule
Summary: Final rule to improve electronic exchange of health data and streamline prior authorization. Requires covered payers to implement Prior Authorization, Provider Access, and Payer‑to‑Payer APIs; send decisions within set timeframes; and include decision rationales.
Healthcare Implications: Reshapes payer automation where AI is embedded: faster, more transparent prior authorization decisions and standardized APIs support audibility of algorithmic determinations and electronic health record integration. Expect vendor updates and tighter governance for model‑assisted utilization management workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Regulation Name: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Certification Program Updates, Algorithm Transparency, and Information Sharing
Summary: Docket entry with supporting materials and public comments for the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy's HTI‑1 rulemaking.
Healthcare Implications: Provides source materials, public input, and references implementers can consult when operationalizing HTI‑1 requirements (e.g., DSI transparency).
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: HTI-1 Final Rule
Summary: Summarizes the Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy's HTI‑1 final rule: adopts the Decision Support Interventions (DSI) certification criterion with transparency for predictive models; adopts USCDI v3; moves to an edition‑less certification framework; updates information blocking; and creates the Insights Condition and Maintenance of Certification.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts EHRs and certified health IT by requiring disclosures for predictive DSIs, improving interoperability, and enabling safer AI integration. Providers and developers must update documentation and compliance practices to meet staged deadlines.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and ASC Payment Systems; Payment for Intensive Outpatient Services and Other Policies
Summary: Finalizes CY 2025 updates to the Physician Fee Schedule and other Medicare Part B policies, including relative value unit updates, coverage/payment clarifications, and Quality Payment Program changes.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts clinician billing and reporting systems that often embed AI‑assisted coding, documentation, and quality tracking. Outpatient digital health and remote monitoring programs should validate any code/payment changes.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and Ambulatory Surgical Center Payment Systems (CY 2023 OPPS/ASC)
Summary: Finalizes CY 2024 updates to Outpatient Prospective Payment System and Ambulatory Surgical Center payment systems, revising payment rates, device pass‑through policies, and OQR/ASCQR measures. Includes clarifications and technical changes across outpatient policy areas.
Healthcare Implications: Outpatient reimbursement and reporting updates affect AI‑assisted coding/analytics and quality measure extraction. Hospitals and ASCs should verify measure logic and documentation templates against CY 2024 rules.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; CY 2024 Physician Fee Schedule and Other Policy Changes
Summary: Companion entry related to CY 2024 Physician Fee Schedule updates and associated Part B policy changes, including Quality Payment Program updates and telehealth provisions.
Healthcare Implications: Similar implications as CY 2024 for Electronic Health Record configurations, AI‑assisted documentation, and quality/telehealth workflows in outpatient settings.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: NOT-OD-23-149 – Use of Generative AI Technologies is Prohibited for the NIH Peer Review Process
Summary: Clarifies that National Institutes of Health prohibits peer reviewers from using generative AI or other automated tools to analyze or draft critiques for NIH peer review, citing confidentiality and integrity requirements. Reinforces existing rules under NOT‑OD‑22‑044.
Healthcare Implications: Affects academic medical centers and researchers involved in National Institutes of Health review. Reinforces data handling and integrity expectations around AI tools in research processes.

Regulation Name: Using Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning in the Development of Drug & Biological Products
Summary: Explores applications of Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning across drug and biological product development (e.g., trial design, data integrity, pharmacovigilance), poses questions for stakeholder feedback, and outlines future policy considerations.
Healthcare Implications: Signals regulatory expectations for AI use in clinical trials and manufacturing; sponsors should plan for transparency, validation, and risk management for AI‑enabled methods.

Regulation Name: Medical Devices; Gastroenterology-Urology Devices; Classification of the Gastrointestinal Lesion Software Detection System
Summary: Docket record and supporting materials associated with the class II classification of the gastrointestinal lesion software detection system (GI Genius).
Healthcare Implications: Provides background documents and references that clinicians and developers can review for technical and regulatory context regarding AI‑assisted endoscopy tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and ASC Payment Systems and Quality Reporting Programs; Organ Acquisition; Rural Emergency Hospitals and Other Policies
Summary: Finalizes CY 2023 updates to the Physician Fee Schedule and other Medicare Part B policies, including valuation changes, telehealth flexibilities, and Quality Payment Program updates.
Healthcare Implications: Continues telehealth and digital health coverage pathways that underpin AI‑enabled remote care and documentation tools. Clinician groups should align AI‑assisted workflows with Quality Payment Program and coding changes.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; CY 2023 Physician Fee Schedule and Other Part B Policies
Summary: Updates hospital outpatient prospective payment system and ambulatory surgical center payment policies for CY 2023, including packaging policies, device pass‑throughs, and outpatient quality reporting changes.
Healthcare Implications: Outpatient coding and quality reporting are touchpoints for AI‑assisted documentation integrity and analytics. Changes may affect adoption and billing pathways for AI‑enabled devices/diagnostics.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: CY 2022 Payment Policies Under the Physician Fee Schedule and Other Changes to Part B Payment Policies...
Summary: Finalizes CY 2022 Physician Fee Schedule updates and associated Part B policy changes, including coding/valuation, telehealth, and Quality Payment Program provisions.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts outpatient care delivery, documentation, and reporting–including AI‑assisted coding and virtual care workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and Ambulatory Surgical Center Payment Systems; Quality Reporting Programs; Price Transparency; Radiation Oncology Model (CY 2021 OPPS/ASC)
Summary: Updates the Medicare hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment System and Ambulatory Surgical Center payments for CY 2022; adjusts packaging, device pass‑throughs, and Outpatient Quality Reporting measures; and finalizes various policy clarifications.
Healthcare Implications: Influences outpatient coding and reimbursement; hospitals and Ambulatory Surgical Centers should update Electronic Health Record charge capture and quality dashboards. AI‑assisted documentation integrity and utilization analytics should reflect CY 2022 OPPS/OQR specifications.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Issuing Body: U.S. FDA; Health Canada; UK MHRA
Regulation Name: Good Machine Learning Practice for Medical Device Development
Summary: Tri‑agency principles for machine learning‑enabled medical devices covering good practices across the total product lifecycle (data, training, testing, deployment, and ongoing learning) to promote safety, effectiveness, and quality.
Healthcare Implications: Device developers and clinical collaborators can use the principles as scaffolding for validation, change management, and transparency – especially for continuously learning models.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services
Regulation Name: Trustworthy AI Playbook
Summary: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office of the Chief AI Officer playbook outlining trustworthy AI concepts, building blocks, and project lifecycle considerations to support ethical, effective, and secure AI solutions across the Department.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a reference for U.S. Department of Health and Human Services‑funded entities and partners; concepts map to governance artifacts (e.g., risk assessments, monitoring plans) used by health systems and vendors working with federal programs.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Hospital Inpatient Prospective Payment Systems and FY 2022 Rates
Summary: Medicare payment policy update for the specified year (Inpatient Prospective Payment System/Outpatient Prospective Payment System), including payment rate recalibrations, quality reporting program changes, and technical corrections.
Healthcare Implications: Expect adjustments to Electronic Health Record-linked reporting and documentation. AI‑enabled analytics used for quality and revenue integrity should reflect updated specifications.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning–Based SaMD Action Plan
Summary: Overview of U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s approach to Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning in Software as a Medical Device, including policy initiatives, guiding principles, and links to guidance and resources. Describes regulatory considerations for AI/ML devices and ongoing engagement activities.
Healthcare Implications: Provides implementers and developers with reference materials to align Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning device development and post‑market practices with U.S. Food and Drug Administration expectations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Regulation Name: AI/ML-Based Software as a Medical Device Action Plan
Summary: Outlines U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s proposed actions to advance oversight of Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning‑based Software as a Medical Device, including issuing guidances (e.g., Predetermined Change Control Plan), fostering Good Machine Learning Practice, and supporting a total product lifecycle approach.
Healthcare Implications: Signals regulatory roadmap that developers and health systems can use to anticipate requirements for AI device validation, updates, and post‑market monitoring.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and ASC Payment Systems; Prior Authorization; COVID-19 Reporting and Other Policies
Summary: Finalizes CY 2021 Physician Fee Schedule updates, including Evaluation and Management coding changes, telehealth expansions, and various Part B policy clarifications.
Healthcare Implications: Evaluation and Management and telehealth changes drive updates to Electronic Health Record templates, coding automation, and AI‑assisted documentation/triage tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare and Medicaid Programs; CY 2021 Physician Fee Schedule and Other Part B Policies
Summary: Companion entry tied to CY 2021 PFS final rule release timing and technical corrections.
Healthcare Implications: Same implications as CY 2021 PFS final rule – coding, telehealth, and AI‑assisted outpatient workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Regulation Name: Medicare and State Health Care Programs: Fraud and Abuse; Revisions to Safe Harbors and Civil Monetary Penalty Rules
Summary: Revises Anti‑Kickback Statute safe harbors and Civil Monetary Penalty rules to protect certain value‑based arrangements, patient engagement tools, and care coordination supports.
Healthcare Implications: Establishes compliance pathways that can encompass digital health and some AI‑enabled services when structured within protected arrangements, reducing fraud‑and‑abuse risk.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Revisions to Payment Policies Under the Physician Fee Schedule and Other Part B for CY 2019
Summary: Finalizes CY 2019 updates to the Medicare Physician Fee Schedule  and other Part B policies, including Evaluation and Management documentation changes, telehealth-related services, and quality program updates. Adjusts relative value units and addresses coding/valuation changes. Includes technical corrections and clarifications across Part B benefits.
Healthcare Implications: Affects outpatient reimbursement and reporting that drive Electronic Health Record configuration, coding workflows, and AI‑assisted documentation. Telehealth provisions help define coverage pathways for virtual services supported by AI screening/triage. Clinician groups should align analytics and quality reporting tools with CY 2019 specifications.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Regulation Name: Medicare Program; Revisions to Payment Policies Under the Physician Fee Schedule and Other Part B for CY 2017
Summary: Finalizes CY 2017 changes to the Medicare PFS and related Part B policies, including updates to the Value Modifier and quality programs, coding/valuation adjustments, and other technical changes. Establishes policies foundational to later Quality Payment Program implementation.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts physician billing and reporting practices that interact with Electronic Health Record templates and AI‑assisted coding. Quality program changes inform measure capture and analytics pipelines used for performance tracking.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors
